name: Skinput
short_description: A bio-acoustic sensing armband that turns the skin of the arm and hand into a tappable input surface.
long_description: Skinput is a CHI 2010 research system by Chris Harrison, Desney Tan, and Dan Morris that uses a wearable array of piezoelectric sensors to detect the acoustic vibrations that finger taps produce as they travel through the body, letting any spot on the arm or hand act as a button. The captured bio-acoustic signals are turned into time-independent acoustic features and classified by a trained support vector machine, which localizes taps to roughly five on-arm locations with over 90% accuracy. Paired with a pico-projector that displays a UI directly on the skin, it matters as an early demonstration of always-available, on-body interaction that treats the body itself as the interface rather than a separate screen.
ai_note: A trained support vector machine classifies bio-acoustic features to localize taps, so a real machine-learning component drives recognition, but it is a narrow classifier rather than the core of the system.

images.caption: The Skinput prototype armband with its bio-acoustic piezoelectric sensor array, used to localize finger taps on the arm.
images.alt_text: A sensor-laden armband worn on a person's upper arm, with a projected interface shown on the forearm below it for tapping.

sources.title: Skinput: Appropriating the Body as an Input Surface
sources.publisher: ACM (Proceedings of the SIGCHI Conference on Human Factors in Computing Systems, CHI 2010)
